dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex and this is my partner Taylor. What made you call 911 today?
I have chest pain and a headache. It started about 30 minutes ago.
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is Serena. I'm in the infirmary.
What day is it today?
Saturday, August the 14th.
And what happened today that made you call 911?
I had chest pain that felt different than before. I had a headache after taking some pills.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain ever, how bad is this chest pain right now?
It feels like an 8.
Do you have any medical conditions we should know about?
Yes, I have high blood pressure, anxiety, and an umbilical hernia.
Can you tell us what happened before you started feeling this chest pain?
I've had heartburn before, but this pain felt totally different. It wasn't linked to eating or lying down.
Do you mind if we check your airway and breathing?
No, go ahead.
You're alert and oriented to four. Are you awake and aware right now?
I'm okay. Just that chest pain and headache are bothering me.
Ma'am, we're going to listen to your breathing now.
You have normal, unlabored breathing. That's good. Your respiratory rate is 16.
Can we check your pulse and blood pressure, ma'am?
Go ahead.
Pulse is 90 and regular.
Blood pressure is 132 over 70.
Oxygen saturation is 96%.
EKG reading is sinus rhythm.
Her glucose is normal.
You were very cooperative and knew exactly who you were. Are you able to move all your extremities?
Yes, I can move my arms and legs.
You mentioned a headache after taking some pills. What exactly did you take?
They gave me three sprays called Nitro. Then I took an Aspirin.
Can you describe the chest pain in a few words?
It's a sharp, central pain that hurts even more when I touch that spot.
Does this pain get worse when you move, or take a breath?
It hurts more when I press on my chest or lie flat.
Have you experienced any dizziness or shortness of breath?
No, I haven't. But I feel a little nauseous when I lie down.
Are you pale or sweating a lot?
No, I don't feel pale. Just a little achy up here.
Anything you want to tell us about your head or neck?
No pain there. I don't remember any injuries.
We're going to attach the ECG now. This won't hurt, but you'll see a few sticky pads on your skin.
We're doing a four-lead ECG first. This is part of our standard process to check your heart rhythm.
The four-lead monitor shows a stable sinus rhythm, but we'll do a more detailed 12-lead now since we noticed some changes in the anterior leads.
First EKG shows a sinus rhythm with some possible heart changes.
You had some improvement with Nitro, but went on to a headache. We already gave you Aspirin to help this.
Pulse is now 96, which is a bit faster than before.
Blood pressure increased slightly to 142 over 97.
The new EKG now shows a sinus tachycardia and some T wave inversions, especially in leads V3 through V6.
Oxygen is still at 99%.
Still normal from the test.
We're going to do a 12-lead EKG now so we can get a more detailed look at your heart, Serena.
This piece of equipment helps us look at different parts of your heart. All in one shot, no pain.
The 12-lead EKG performed at 01:17 showed T wave inversions and ST depression in the anterior leads, V3 through V6, as well as in lead I. Strong signs are pointing to possible anterolateral strain.
We're going to start an IV line in your arm to give medicine or fluids if needed.
Needle placed in a vein of the right forearm. You now have an 18-gauge IV in place.
We're following protocol for suspected heart-related chest pain.
Let's check your pulse again. It's still at 96.
BP remains 142 over 97. Oxygen and glucose also look steady.
We just repeated the 12-lead EKG. The findings mirror what we saw earlier but the T waves are still inverted in some leads. No STE or ectopy.
We are taking you to VCU Health in Richmond. Is that the nearest appropriate facility?
Yes, that's the closest.
We need to move you to the stretcher now. Would you be more comfortable standing up and pivoting with us?
Thanks for helping. We've secured you now and you're fully safe to take.
Pt is stable for transportation. Initial EKG shows changes. 12-lead still shows possible anterolateral strain and T wave inversions.
Pt is alert and oriented to time, place, and situation. No diaphoresis observed. Has a history of hypertension, anxiety, and umbilical hernia.
She was given three Nitros with improvement then had headache. We're treating with standard protocol for suspected cardiac chest pain.
Her GCS is 15. She reported that she had chest pain different from GERD. T waves showed some inversion, so we did IV and EKG as part of protocol.
Pt will be taken to VCU emergency room 13 for further care.
Let me know if anything changes. I'll monitor you during transport.
We should get there quickly. You'll be admitted to the ED for further evaluation.
Just to recap, was there anything that caused this chest pain? Like stress or physical activity?
I was resting. Just sitting in the infirmary when it started.
Have you had any other symptoms like back pain or arm pain?
Only chest and head.
We noticed nothing listed, but just to be clear, are you allergic to any medications?
No, but I do avoid Ibuprofen and Oxycodone.
What medications do you usually take at home?
I take Cymbalta and Trazodone.
Thanks for going through all of this, Serena. You're doing great.
If anything changes at all, especially worsening pain or any shortness of breath, let me know right away.
We're on the road now and heading to VCU emergency room 13.
Your vitals remain stable. EKG also unchanged.
Let's confirm medications one more time. We gave Aspirin and Nitro three times.
IV line is still patent, and we're ready to administer anything if needed during transit.
Let me recheck your pain level. Is it still an 8 or has it changed?
Still around an 8. It's not better or worse.
And where exactly is it if we draw a picture of your chest?
Right around the center, maybe near the sternum.
We're almost there. I'm going to check your pulse one more time.
Still at 96. Let's keep you stable until arrival.
We're wrapping up the last checks. Your vital signs are stable and trending.
The nurse, upon arrival in ED room 13, will have the full picture and how we assessed you.
Thank you, Serena, for letting us help. I'm going to alert the hospital as we arrive.
Full report being handed over with the proper notes and EKG copies. EMT notes, vitals, and patient history included.
Pt is alert, IV in place, all vitals within range. We'll go in now for the handoff.
Serena, you're in good hands now. I'm just going to make one last check for anything else.
All clear. ED staff is ready and waiting.
Let's move her into the room. Process complete.
All done for this shift. Patient cared for per protocol 2-1: medical - chest pain - cardiac suspected.
